```{r bt-cumret-chart, fig.alt="Interactive plotly cumulative return chart with stress period shading"}
  plotly::layout(hovermode = "x unified",
                 legend = list(orientation = "h", y = 1.08))
```

## 8.4 Monthly Returns Heatmap

```{r bt-heatmap, fig.height=7, fig.alt="Monthly returns heatmap year by month"}
monthly_ret_tbl <- bt_nav |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    year  = lubridate::year(date),
    month = lubridate::month(date, label = TRUE, abbr = TRUE)
  ) |>
  dplyr::group_by(year, month) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    m_ret = dplyr::last(nav) / dplyr::first(nav) - 1,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot2::ggplot(monthly_ret_tbl,
                ggplot2::aes(x = month, y = factor(year), fill = m_ret)) +
  ggplot2::geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
  ggplot2::geom_text(
    ggplot2::aes(
      label  = scales::percent(m_ret, accuracy = 0.1),
      colour = abs(m_ret) > 0.06
    ),
    size = 2.6, show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_fill_gradient2(
    low      = "#dc2626", mid = "white", high = "#16a34a",
    midpoint = 0,
    labels   = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1)
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = "white", `FALSE` = "grey20")) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_discrete(limits = rev) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = "Monthly Returns Heatmap — Long-Short Strategy (Training)",
    x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = "Monthly\nReturn"
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  ggplot2::theme(
    plot.title       = ggplot2::element_text(face = "bold"),
    panel.grid       = ggplot2::element_blank(),
    axis.text.y      = ggplot2::element_text(size = 8),
    legend.position  = "right"
  )
```

## 8.5 Drawdown Chart & Long/Short Contribution

```{r bt-drawdown-contrib, fig.height=8, fig.alt="Patchwork of drawdown and long/short contribution charts"}
# ── Drawdown ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
drawdown_tbl <- bt_nav |>
  dplyr::arrange(date) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    running_max = cummax(nav),
    drawdown    = nav / running_max - 1
  )

p_dd <- ggplot2::ggplot(drawdown_tbl,
                         ggplot2::aes(x = date, y = drawdown)) +
  ggplot2::geom_area(fill = "#fca5a5", alpha = 0.6) +
  ggplot2::geom_line(colour = "#dc2626", linewidth = 0.5) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  ggplot2::scale_x_date(date_breaks = "3 years", date_labels = "%Y") +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = "Strategy Drawdown — Training Period",
    x = NULL, y = "Drawdown from Peak"
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  ggplot2::theme(plot.title       = ggplot2::element_text(face = "bold"),
                 panel.grid.minor = ggplot2::element_blank())

# ── Long / Short monthly contribution breakdown ───────────────────────────────
contrib_tbl <- signal_at_rebal |>
  dplyr::left_join(
    train_data |> dplyr::filter(symbol %in% SECTORS),
    by = c("symbol", "date")
  ) |>
  dplyr::group_by(date, position) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    contrib = mean(
      dplyr::if_else(position != 0L,
        sign(position) * (price / dplyr::lag(price, default = NA) - 1),
        0),
      na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(contrib)) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    book = dplyr::case_when(
      position ==  1L ~ "Long",
      position == -1L ~ "Short",
      TRUE            ~ "Cash"
    )
  ) |>
  dplyr::filter(book != "Cash")

p_contrib <- ggplot2::ggplot(
  contrib_tbl,
  ggplot2::aes(x = date, y = contrib, fill = book)
) +
  ggplot2::geom_col(position = "stack", width = 20) +
  ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = c(Long = "#16a34a", Short = "#dc2626")) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 0.1)) +
  ggplot2::scale_x_date(date_breaks = "3 years", date_labels = "%Y") +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = "Long / Short Monthly Contribution — Training Period",
    x = NULL, y = "Average Daily Return Contribution",
    fill = "Book"
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  ggplot2::theme(plot.title       = ggplot2::element_text(face = "bold"),
                 panel.grid.minor = ggplot2::element_blank(),
                 legend.position  = "top")

# ── patchwork layout ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
p_dd / p_contrib +
  patchwork::plot_annotation(
    title = "Risk & Attribution — Training Period Backtest",
    theme = ggplot2::theme(plot.title = ggplot2::element_text(face = "bold",
                                                               size = 13))
  )
```

---

# 9. Out-of-Sample Test {#sec-oos}

All model parameters — Kalman noise variances $W_{i,k}$ and $V_{i,k}$, the baseline threshold $\theta_{i,k}^{\text{base}}$, the feature scaling statistics, and the cluster centroid mapping — were estimated exclusively on the training period (June 2000 – December 2024) and are carried forward without modification. No re-estimation occurs on test data.

## 9.1 OOS Signal Generation

```{r oos-rebal-dates}
# ── End-of-month rebalancing dates within test period ─────────────────────────
rebal_dates_oos <- seq(
  lubridate::ceiling_date(TEST_START, "month"),
  TEST_END,
  by = "month"
) |>
  lubridate::rollback()

# ── Re-build feature matrix at each OOS rebalancing date ─────────────────────
# Uses all data up to each rd (training + OOS so far) — no look-ahead.
# Kalman W/V, theta_base, scaling params, and cluster centroids are all frozen
# from the training period; only the feature window advances.
oos_cluster_features <- purrr::map_dfr(SECTORS, function(sym) {
  sym_df <- test_data |> dplyr::filter(symbol == sym)
  purrr::map_dfr(rebal_dates_oos, function(rd) {
    # Use all data up to rd (training + test available so far) — no look-ahead
    full_sym <- dplyr::bind_rows(
      train_data |> dplyr::filter(symbol == sym),
      test_data  |> dplyr::filter(symbol == sym, date <= rd)
    )
    build_cluster_features(full_sym, rd)
  }) |>
    dplyr::mutate(symbol = sym)
}) |>
  dplyr::filter(dplyr::if_all(tidyselect::where(is.numeric), ~ !is.na(.x)))

# ── Predict cluster using frozen training centroids ───────────────────────────
# Bake OOS features with training recipe (no re-prepping)
oos_baked <- recipes::bake(clust_rec, new_data = oos_cluster_features |>
                             dplyr::select(roll_ret, roll_vol, roll_sr,
                                           mean_rsi, mean_macd))

# Assign to nearest centroid (Euclidean distance to km_fit$centers)
oos_cluster_num <- apply(oos_baked, 1L, function(row) {
  dists <- apply(km_fit$centers, 1L, function(ctr) sqrt(sum((row - ctr)^2)))
  which.min(dists)
})

oos_cluster_labels <- oos_cluster_features |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    cluster_num   = oos_cluster_num,
    cluster_label = dplyr::recode(
      as.character(cluster_num),
      !!!stats::setNames(label_map$cluster_label,
                         as.character(label_map$cluster_num))
    ),
    date = rebal_date
  ) |>
  dplyr::select(symbol, date, cluster_label)

# ── Build OOS signal snapshot at each rebalancing date ───────────────────────
signal_at_rebal_oos <- purrr::map_dfr(rebal_dates_oos, function(rd) {
  signals_tbl |>
    dplyr::filter(symbol %in% SECTORS, period == "test", date <= rd) |>
    dplyr::group_by(symbol) |>
    dplyr::slice_tail(n = 1L) |>
    dplyr::ungroup() |>
    dplyr::select(-cluster_label) |>
    dplyr::left_join(
      oos_cluster_labels |> dplyr::filter(date == rd),
      by = "symbol"
    ) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      date         = rd,
      entry_signal = dplyr::case_when(
        cluster_label == "Growth"    & !is.na(delta) &
          delta >  theta_adaptive  ~ "long_entry",
        cluster_label == "Defensive" & !is.na(delta) &
          delta < -theta_adaptive  ~ "short_entry",
        TRUE                       ~ "no_entry"
      ),
      position = dplyr::case_when(
        entry_signal == "long_entry"  ~  1L,
        entry_signal == "short_entry" ~ -1L,
        TRUE                          ~  0L
      )
    )
})
```

## 9.2 OOS NAV Computation

```{r oos-nav}
# ── Run rebalance_portfolio() over OOS period ─────────────────────────────────
n_rebal_oos  <- length(rebal_dates_oos)
nav_list_oos <- vector("list", n_rebal_oos - 1L)

for (i in seq_len(n_rebal_oos - 1L)) {
  nav_list_oos[[i]] <- rebalance_portfolio(
    rebal_date      = rebal_dates_oos[i],
    next_rebal_date = rebal_dates_oos[i + 1L],
    signals_tbl     = signal_at_rebal_oos,
    price_data      = test_data |> dplyr::filter(symbol %in% SECTORS),
    prev_nav        = if (i == 1L) 1
                      else dplyr::last(nav_list_oos[[i - 1L]]$nav)
  )
}

oos_nav <- dplyr::bind_rows(nav_list_oos) |>
  dplyr::arrange(date) |>
  dplyr::mutate(cum_ret = nav / dplyr::first(nav) - 1)

# ── Long-only OOS ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
signal_lo_oos <- signal_at_rebal_oos |>
  dplyr::mutate(position = dplyr::if_else(position == -1L, 0L, position))

nav_list_oos_lo <- vector("list", n_rebal_oos - 1L)
for (i in seq_len(n_rebal_oos - 1L)) {
  nav_list_oos_lo[[i]] <- rebalance_portfolio(
    rebal_date      = rebal_dates_oos[i],
    next_rebal_date = rebal_dates_oos[i + 1L],
    signals_tbl     = signal_lo_oos,
    price_data      = test_data |> dplyr::filter(symbol %in% SECTORS),
    prev_nav        = if (i == 1L) 1
                      else dplyr::last(nav_list_oos_lo[[i - 1L]]$nav)
  )
}

oos_nav_lo <- dplyr::bind_rows(nav_list_oos_lo) |>
  dplyr::arrange(date) |>
  dplyr::mutate(cum_ret = nav / dplyr::first(nav) - 1)

# ── SPY OOS benchmark ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
spy_nav_oos <- test_data |>
  dplyr::filter(symbol == "SPY",
                date >= min(oos_nav$date), date <= max(oos_nav$date)) |>
  dplyr::arrange(date) |>
  dplyr::mutate(nav = price / dplyr::first(price), cum_ret = nav - 1)
```

## 9.3 Performance Metrics

```{r oos-metrics}
# ── OOS metrics using same compute_metrics() helper from Section 8 ─────────────
oos_metrics <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  compute_metrics(oos_nav,    "Long-Short (OOS)", spy_nav_oos),
  compute_metrics(oos_nav_lo, "Long-Only (OOS)",  spy_nav_oos),
  compute_metrics(spy_nav_oos,"SPY (OOS)",         spy_nav_oos)
)

# ── Side-by-side training vs OOS ─────────────────────────────────────────────
train_metrics_compare <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  compute_metrics(bt_nav,    "Long-Short", spy_nav),
  compute_metrics(spy_nav,   "SPY",        spy_nav)
) |>
  dplyr::rename_with(~ paste0(.x, " (Train)"), -Strategy)

oos_metrics_compare <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  compute_metrics(oos_nav,    "Long-Short", spy_nav_oos),
  compute_metrics(spy_nav_oos,"SPY",        spy_nav_oos)
) |>
  dplyr::rename_with(~ paste0(.x, " (OOS)"), -Strategy)

dplyr::left_join(train_metrics_compare, oos_metrics_compare, by = "Strategy") |>
  gt::gt() |>
  gt::tab_header(
    title    = "Training vs Out-of-Sample Performance Comparison",
    subtitle = "Train: Jun 2000–Dec 2024  |  OOS: Jan 2025–Mar 2026"
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_percent(
    columns = tidyselect::contains(c("Return", "Vol", "Drawdown", "Win")),
    decimals = 2
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_number(
    columns = tidyselect::contains(c("Sharpe", "Info")),
    decimals = 3
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style     = gt::cell_fill(color = "#dcfce7"),
    locations = gt::cells_body(rows = Strategy == "Long-Short")
  ) |>
  gt::tab_spanner(label = "Training Period",
                  columns = tidyselect::ends_with("(Train)")) |>
  gt::tab_spanner(label = "Out-of-Sample Period",
                  columns = tidyselect::ends_with("(OOS)")) |>
  gt::sub_missing(missing_text = "—") |>
  gt::opt_table_font(font = gt::google_font("IBM Plex Sans")) |>
  gt::opt_stylize(style = 1, color = "blue")
```

## 9.4 OOS Cumulative Returns

```{r oos-cumret, fig.alt="Interactive OOS cumulative return chart vs SPY"}
oos_long <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  oos_nav    |> dplyr::transmute(date, cum_ret, series = "Long-Short (OOS)"),
  oos_nav_lo |> dplyr::transmute(date, cum_ret, series = "Long-Only (OOS)"),
  spy_nav_oos|> dplyr::transmute(date, cum_ret, series = "SPY (OOS)")
)

p_oos_cum <- ggplot2::ggplot(
  oos_long,
  ggplot2::aes(x = date, y = cum_ret, colour = series)
) +
  ggplot2::geom_line(linewidth = 0.7) +
  ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(`Long-Short (OOS)` = "#2563EB",
               `Long-Only (OOS)`  = "#16a34a",
               `SPY (OOS)`        = "#6b7280")
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  ggplot2::scale_x_date(date_breaks = "3 months", date_labels = "%b %Y") +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title   = "OOS Cumulative Returns — Jan 2025 to Mar 2026",
    x = NULL, y = "Cumulative Return", colour = NULL
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  ggplot2::theme(plot.title       = ggplot2::element_text(face = "bold"),
                 panel.grid.minor = ggplot2::element_blank(),
                 legend.position  = "top")

plotly::ggplotly(p_oos_cum, tooltip = c("colour", "x", "y")) |>
  plotly::layout(hovermode = "x unified",
                 legend = list(orientation = "h", y = 1.1))
```

## 9.5 Training vs OOS Side-by-Side

```{r oos-vs-train, fig.height=5, fig.alt="Patchwork of training and OOS cumulative return charts side by side"}
# ── Normalise training returns for visual comparability ───────────────────────
train_long <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  bt_nav  |> dplyr::transmute(date, cum_ret, series = "Long-Short"),
  spy_nav |> dplyr::transmute(date, cum_ret, series = "SPY")
)

make_cumret_panel <- function(df, title_str) {
  ggplot2::ggplot(df, ggplot2::aes(x = date, y = cum_ret, colour = series)) +
    ggplot2::geom_line(linewidth = 0.6) +
    ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(
      values = c(`Long-Short` = "#2563EB", SPY = "#6b7280",
                 `Long-Short (OOS)` = "#2563EB", `SPY (OOS)` = "#6b7280")
    ) +
    ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
    ggplot2::scale_x_date(date_labels = "%Y") +
    ggplot2::labs(title = title_str, x = NULL, y = "Cumulative Return",
                  colour = NULL) +
    ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
    ggplot2::theme(plot.title       = ggplot2::element_text(face = "bold"),
                   panel.grid.minor = ggplot2::element_blank(),
                   legend.position  = "bottom")
}

p_train_panel <- make_cumret_panel(train_long,
                                   "Training (Jun 2000 – Dec 2024)")
p_oos_panel   <- make_cumret_panel(
  oos_long |>
    dplyr::filter(series %in% c("Long-Short (OOS)", "SPY (OOS)")) |>
    dplyr::mutate(series = stringr::str_remove(series, " \\(OOS\\)")),
  "Out-of-Sample (Jan 2025 – Mar 2026)"
)

p_train_panel | p_oos_panel
```

## 9.6 OOS Monthly Returns Heatmap

```{r oos-heatmap, fig.alt="OOS monthly returns heatmap"}
oos_monthly <- oos_nav |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    year  = lubridate::year(date),
    month = lubridate::month(date, label = TRUE, abbr = TRUE)
  ) |>
  dplyr::group_by(year, month) |>
  dplyr::summarise(m_ret = dplyr::last(nav) / dplyr::first(nav) - 1,
                   .groups = "drop")

ggplot2::ggplot(oos_monthly,
                ggplot2::aes(x = month, y = factor(year), fill = m_ret)) +
  ggplot2::geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  ggplot2::geom_text(
    ggplot2::aes(
      label  = scales::percent(m_ret, accuracy = 0.1),
      colour = abs(m_ret) > 0.04
    ),
    size = 3.2, show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#dc2626", mid = "white", high = "#16a34a", midpoint = 0,
    labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1)
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = "white",
                                          `FALSE` = "grey20")) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_discrete(limits = rev) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = "Monthly Returns Heatmap — OOS Period",
    x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = "Monthly\nReturn"
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  ggplot2::theme(plot.title    = ggplot2::element_text(face = "bold"),
                 panel.grid    = ggplot2::element_blank())
```

## 9.7 Honest Assessment

The out-of-sample period (January 2025 – March 2026) presents a genuinely short window — fifteen months — which limits the statistical power of any performance comparison. Several structural factors are worth examining honestly.

**Cluster stability in the OOS period.** The k-means centroids were frozen at end-2024. If the cross-sectional dispersion of sector returns compressed or reversed in 2025 — as might occur in a late-cycle or policy-driven environment — the frozen centroids will mis-classify regimes. The stability analysis in Section 5 showed that post-COVID centroids already drifted relative to the pre-2020 period; a further drift in 2025 would compound this. Any month where the average silhouette width falls below 0.25 should be treated as a low-confidence cluster assignment, and positions taken in those months should be interpreted cautiously.

**Monthly rebalancing lag.** The strategy is structurally slow to react. Entry signals are only evaluated at month-end; exit signals via the innovation z-score operate daily but require 63 consecutive days of innovation data to produce a reliable z-score. In fast-moving markets — a sudden Fed pivot, a geopolitical shock — a position can be held through a significant drawdown before the exit triggers. The 2025 environment, characterised by elevated rate uncertainty and occasional sharp sector rotations, is precisely the regime where this lag is most costly.

**Value added by the short book.** The comparison between the long-short and long-only versions in the OOS period directly tests whether shorting the Defensive cluster added or subtracted value. If Defensive sectors (XLU, XLP, XLRE) performed well in 2025 — which is plausible in a risk-off or rate-cut environment — the short book would have detracted. The metrics table above shows the directional result; the long-only version is the appropriate counterfactual.

**What would need to be true for the strategy to work OOS.** The strategy's positive expectation relies on three conditions holding: (1) sector return dispersion remains wide enough for K-means to find distinct clusters, (2) the Kalman filtered signal leads price moves rather than lagging them, and (3) the short book's Defensive ETFs genuinely underperform during Growth regimes. All three are empirical claims that should be re-examined with each year of new data — not maintained by assumption.

---

# 10. Circle Back {#sec-circle-back}

## 10.1 What the Strategy Learned

The document opened with a single question: why should an investor pay for active management instead of simply holding SPY? Nine sections later, the honest answer is that the strategy earns that justification in some market conditions and not others — and understanding the boundary between those conditions is itself the most valuable output of the analysis.

The clustering exercise revealed something non-trivial about the structure of sector returns. The Growth / Neutral / Defensive taxonomy was not imposed arbitrarily; it emerged from the data. The k-means algorithm consistently separated a high-return, moderate-volatility group of sectors (technology, consumer discretionary, communication services) from a low-return, low-volatility group (utilities, real estate, consumer staples) with an intermediate cluster in between. This structure proved reasonably stable across the pre-COVID sub-period (2000–2019) and the post-COVID sub-period (2020–2024), though the centroids drifted — the post-COVID Growth cluster moved to higher return space, and the Defensive cluster became more tightly defined. The three-cluster solution found a genuine economic signal: sectors do rotate across regimes, and that rotation is partially predictable from lagged price behaviour.

The Kalman filter served a purpose beyond mere smoothing. The innovation sequence — the filter's one-step-ahead prediction error — carried information that the raw technical indicators did not. When the z-score of the innovation sequence spiked beyond two standard deviations, the filter was encountering observations that its learned model of the world did not anticipate. In the training period, the majority of these spikes preceded or coincided with cluster flips, suggesting that the innovation exit captured regime transitions earlier than the monthly cluster re-assignment could. This is the strategy's most technically differentiated feature: it uses the internal mechanics of the filter, not just its output, as a risk signal.

## 10.2 When Clustering Fails

No statistical model is unconditionally valid, and k-means clustering on sector ETFs is particularly fragile under three identifiable conditions.

The first is extreme volatility. When the CBOE VIX index exceeds approximately 40 — as it did in September 2008, March 2020, and briefly in early 2022 — cross-sector correlations converge sharply toward 1.0. Every sector falls simultaneously, and the five-dimensional feature space that normally separates Growth from Defensive collapses to a single dimension: magnitude of loss. In this environment, the average silhouette width typically falls below 0.25, signalling that cluster assignments are essentially random. A strategy that enters short positions in Defensive ETFs during a market panic will find that those ETFs also collapse, destroying the thesis of the short book.

The second failure condition is structural breaks in sector relationships. The AI revolution that accelerated after 2022 fundamentally altered the return dynamics of the technology sector and, by extension, its correlations with every other sector. A clustering model trained on data through 2024 implicitly assumes that XLK's relationship to XLU in 2025 resembles its historical relationship. If that assumption fails — because technology has become a macro factor rather than a sector — the cluster centroids are anchored to a world that no longer exists.

The third condition is regime speed. The strategy rebalances monthly and evaluates cluster flips monthly. A regime transition that unfolds over days rather than weeks — the March 2020 COVID crash took approximately 23 trading days from peak to trough — will generate a cluster flip signal only after the damage is done. The innovation z-score exit provides some daily-frequency protection, but it requires 63 days of innovation history to compute a reliable z-score, meaning the first two months of any new regime are partially blind.

## 10.3 Limitations

Several limitations constrain the practical interpretation of the results and would need to be addressed before any live implementation.

Transaction costs and market impact are absent from the backtest. The strategy rebalances eleven ETF positions monthly and may exit individual positions intra-month on innovation signals. In liquid ETFs this cost is small per trade, but across a 24-year backtest with frequent rebalancing it is not negligible. A conservative estimate of 5 basis points per one-way trade — low given the liquidity of SPDR sector ETFs — would meaningfully reduce the reported Sharpe ratio, particularly in periods with high turnover.

Short selling is treated as costless. In practice, the borrow cost for sector ETFs varies from negligible to prohibitive depending on short interest at any given time. During the periods when the strategy most wants to be short Defensive ETFs — specifically during bear markets — those ETFs may carry elevated borrow costs precisely because other investors have the same idea. The strategy's information ratio relative to SPY should be understood as an upper bound that will compress in any realistic implementation.

The single-model structure creates concentration risk. K-means with $k = 3$ is one of many possible clustering approaches. Different distance metrics, different initialisation seeds (though controlled here with `set.seed(451)`), or different numbers of clusters would produce different regime assignments. The strategy does not quantify uncertainty across clustering models — it commits to a single solution and acts on it. A more robust implementation would average signals across multiple cluster specifications and reduce position size in months where the solutions disagree.

Finally, the look-ahead audit: the most dangerous form of bias in backtesting is subtle. The Kalman parameters $W_{i,k}$ and $V_{i,k}$ were estimated on training data and frozen — this is correct. The feature scaling statistics and cluster centroids were similarly frozen — this is correct. The one remaining risk is the `theta_base` calculation, which uses the median absolute delta from the training period and is applied to test-period deltas. This is a valid use of training-period statistics applied to test data, but it assumes that the distribution of deltas is stationary across the two periods. If the test-period filtered signals are substantially more volatile than the training period — possible in a high-uncertainty macro environment — `theta_base` will be set too low and generate excessive false entries.

## 10.4 Inversion: Five Ways This Strategy Fails

Inversion is more revealing than optimisation. Rather than asking how to maximise returns, the more useful question is: what would have to be true for this strategy to reliably destroy capital? Five conditions are sufficient.

First, a persistent low-dispersion regime in which all eleven sector ETFs move together for an extended period. In such an environment the cross-sectional variance of the feature matrix collapses, silhouette widths approach zero, and the three-cluster solution becomes arbitrary. The strategy would generate random signals, and transaction costs would erode capital steadily.

Second, a sustained inverted-return relationship between the Growth and Defensive clusters. If utilities and consumer staples deliver higher returns than technology and consumer discretionary over a multi-year period — which occurred partially during the 2022 rate-hike cycle — the short book becomes the return source while the long book destroys value. The strategy would lose on both sides if the cluster labels fail to update fast enough to reflect the inversion.

Third, a structural break in the Kalman filter's learned model. If the statistical relationship between indicator levels and their filtered states changes — because high-frequency trading algorithms have permanently altered the autocorrelation structure of RSI or MACD — the filter's innovation sequence will be persistently non-zero and will trigger false exits constantly, churning the portfolio and incurring transaction costs without protective benefit.

Fourth, concentrated crowding in sector ETF trades. If a sufficiently large number of quantitative managers implement sector rotation strategies on the same eleven ETFs using similar signals, the entry and exit points the strategy identifies will already be priced in. The strategy profits on the existence of a systematic relationship between signals and subsequent returns; if that relationship is arbitraged away by crowding, the edge disappears even if the signals remain technically correct.

Fifth, regulatory or structural changes to the short-selling market. A sustained restriction on borrowing Defensive ETFs — analogous to the short-sale restrictions imposed on financial stocks in 2008 — would prevent the strategy from expressing its Defensive-cluster thesis, leaving it holding only a long book with no hedge. This is not a theoretical risk; it has happened in precisely the regime type that triggers the short-book signals.

## 10.5 What Would Change in a Rebuild

If building this strategy from scratch with the benefit of hindsight, three changes would improve robustness without sacrificing the core thesis.

The first is regime uncertainty quantification. Instead of a single k-means solution, a Gaussian Mixture Model would provide cluster membership probabilities rather than hard assignments. A month in which XLK has 0.9 probability of belonging to the Growth cluster is fundamentally different from a month in which the probability is 0.6. Position sizes should scale with this confidence. Scott Page's concept of diversity in forecasting models applies directly here: averaging across multiple clustering approaches — k-means, GMM, hierarchical clustering — would reduce the variance of the regime signal without necessarily reducing its mean.

The second change is signal frequency. Monthly rebalancing is appropriate for a strategy whose primary driver is regime-level behaviour, but the intra-month innovation exit points to the value of a mixed-frequency architecture. The entry and exit rules could be evaluated at weekly frequency — retaining the monthly cluster assignment as the regime prior but allowing weekly reassessment of whether the delta signal still supports the position. This would reduce the lag cost identified in Sections 9 and 10.2 without abandoning the deliberate, low-turnover character of the strategy.

The third change is an explicit regime-quality filter. A silhouette width below 0.25 should suppress all trading signals for that month, returning the strategy to a cash position regardless of what the cluster label says. Many models agree: if the clustering is ambiguous, the signal is uninformative, and the correct action is to do nothing. The course framework's emphasis on knowing when your model is not applicable is more valuable than any specific model improvement.

## 10.6 Connection to the Course Framework

This document followed the Frame → Sketch → Implement → Circle Back structure throughout. Section 1 framed the problem — why active management, and under what conditions does it create value. Sections 2 and 7 sketched the decision architecture before any code was written, forcing a commitment to the rules in plain language and mathematical notation before seeing any results. Sections 3 through 6 implemented the strategy sequentially, with each section producing outputs that fed the next without crossing the look-ahead boundary. Sections 8 and 9 evaluated the implementation honestly against an observable benchmark, reporting both the successes and the failures. This section circles back to the original frame.

The course's many-models philosophy is visible throughout the strategy's architecture. The Kalman filter is one model of how indicators evolve; the k-means clustering is a separate model of how regimes partition the sector space; the innovation z-score is a third model of when the first model is failing. No single model is trusted unconditionally. When they agree — delta above threshold, cluster in Growth, innovation z-score within normal range — the signal is strong. When they disagree — cluster says Growth but innovation z-score is spiking — the exit overrides the entry, which is the correct priority ordering under uncertainty.

The final answer to the opening question is this: the strategy justifies active management over holding SPY during periods of high cross-sector dispersion, stable regime structure, and moderate volatility — broadly, the kind of market that prevailed for much of the 2003–2007 and 2013–2019 periods. It does not justify active management during crises, during structural breaks, or during the first fifteen months of a new macro regime when the cluster model is most likely to be stale. An intellectually honest investment mandate would state this explicitly, suspend trading when silhouette widths fall below the quality threshold, and measure performance only over the regimes in which the strategy was designed to operate.

---

# References {#sec-references}

<!-- ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
     REFERENCES
     Required citations:
       • Kalman (1960) — original Kalman filter paper
       • Harvey (1989) — Time Series Models, state space in econometrics
       • Durbin & Koopman (2012) — State Space Methods by State Space
       • Hartigan & Wong (1979) — K-means algorithm
       • Avellaneda & Lee (2010) — stat arb, state space in finance
     ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── -->
